concept_ref: §3 row 3 + §1 'sub-condition can never change value across all tests — masked by the others'

scenario.title: Admin Override With Negation
scenario.narrative: A panel is shown when the user is NOT an admin or has an explicit override. The QA engineer claims Branch Condition coverage is met with three tests. Read carefully — the negation is the trap.

claim: Which is the STRONGEST coverage level the test set below actually satisfies?

options.label: Branch Condition (each condition T and F)
MCDC — independence pairs for both conditions

hints: Read each condition at the SOURCE level: A is the literal 'is_admin', not 'not is_admin'. Branch Condition coverage requires A and B each to be observed as T and F. Re-read Law Card 4.

Tally for A and B independently. A: F in T1, T in T2 and T3 ✓. B: F in T1 and T3, T in T2 ✓. Both conditions are observed T and F — BC is satisfied. Now ask: is MCDC also satisfied? Try to build an independence pair for each condition.

Solution — Decision: T1+T2 give true, T3 gives false → ✓. BC: each of A and B observed T and F → ✓. MCDC for A: need a pair where ONLY A flips and outcome flips. T1↔T3 flips A and outcome → ✓ for A. MCDC for B: need only-B-flips with outcome flip. Short-circuit on (!A) when A=F masks B; no valid pair exists in T1..T3. Strongest satisfied = BRANCH CONDITION.

seeded_faults.description: The override flag is read from the wrong variable; the bug is masked when is_admin is FALSE because short-circuit evaluation skips the second operand.

misconceptions.explanation_md: Negations and short-circuit operators trick learners into thinking that if every condition appears as T and F at the source level, then everything stronger is automatically satisfied too. BC is satisfied here, but B can never independently flip the outcome when A=False — exactly the kind of masking effect MCDC is designed to surface (§5.3.6, Annex C.2.3.6).
